item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand what this has to do with the Pattern Screamers, but I'm still trying to figure out what they are. Is there a hub or something for them? I've been looking through pages with the Pattern Screamers tag bur most of them seem to assume you're reading in a different order. It would be helpful if you could add links to articles you're referencing, in case people are reading this one first.

item.title: SCP-5170: +1
item.content:encoded: This is pretty long, but it works so well. I never got bored even once, and it was nice how a lot of stuff clicked perfectly, like pulling an already completed puzzle from a closet. It certainly was a bit confusing especially early on, but that's to be expected from a legal nightmare skip and it definitely clears up as it goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
as someone who wasted too much of his life negotiating contracts I found this absolutely compelling. +1

tiny nit
tin ʎnit

The checked form box in the final part uses a character, 🗹, that isn't widely supported, having been introduced as a non-standard addition in Unicode 7 i.e. very recently. Many browsers will render a placeholder instead. By pure coincidence this looks like an unchecked ballot box on some, but could be uglier on others, and I noticed particularly using Firefox before being distracted by a bear.

Can I suggest using ☒ instead? It should render correctly on most/all platforms due to inclusion since Unicode 1.1.

item.title: Operational Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Per instructions of the Licensing Captain, I have removed the images immediately.

For those curious: while government logos are under public domain, their usage is restricted by laws other than copyright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 I liked this when I crit it, and I can safely says it’s improved and more streamlined than when I first saw it.

As an attorney, I would say quite a bit of this is very grounded in appropriate language. And the dry legal terminology serves to hide the wackiness of the anomaly.

Enjoyed it thoroughly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty entertaining piece, and has some engaging commentary and narrative progression. I didn't read any of the hidden text, so I'm glad the writing still holds up without it.

I will echo concerns mentioned earlier: while the dryness is intentional and makes for good tone, the visual profile (continuous text with no images, or other elements to break it up) was a bit hard to read. I would suggest going through and trimming out sentences not strictly necessary, and peppering in other elements (e.g. visual exhibits, blockquotes, tables) to make it easier on the eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a solid debut! The tidbits of social commentary weaved throughout do well to ground an otherwise abstract piece. Here's to many more successful SCPs!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. It would be +2 if that wouldn’t get me Rule 11’d.

Great job. You did well with something that should have been done long before.

I appreciate your fidelity to real legal concepts and norms, and that you are making a wider audience aware of the kafkaesque nightmare that is immigration court. The attention to detail is deeply respected over here.

the ONLY thing I suggest changing is so minor:
+ show block
– hide block

on the primary page, use of “comprised of” is technically grammatically incorrect - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comprised_of - and while virtually no one knows or cares about that rule, I think being mindful of it can help if you find yourself in front of a pedantic judge in the future. happy practicing.

item.title: Re: Pattern Screamer, esq.
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!

It's hidden better than most because of the listpages, so checking source from Wikidot won't get you anywhere. You either have to check the source of the fragment pages, or inspect element and look right below <div class="list-pages-box"> for each of the three iterations.

If that's too much effort, the lines are:

I was a crease in the retinas behind a stillborn child's eyes.
When she looked at me, she saw what that corpse could have been.
As she spoke of me, I made it true.

item.title: Re: Pattern Screamer, esq.
item.content:encoded:
Funny. I read through this, enjoyed tmhe weird legal system affecting SCP, and upvoted, and not once did the idea of pattern screamers pop into my head. When I finished it, I was under the impression that it was something created from the hopes and dreams of all of those lawyers who really want to make the world better, who wish to make laws more just and get things done but are prevented by the very system in which they operate.

I checked the page source but I couldn't find anything hidden in the formatting, so if it's hidden in there it's hidden in there.

Now of course none of this is to say that I think this is a bad pattern screamer skip because I didn't realize it was pattern screamer skip the entire time. I think it's a perfect pattern screamer skip because of that. So, so many mediocre skips are out there are boring unless you "find the big secret" which is usually just white text anyways. This has managed to be interesting all on its own, so much so that me not realizing there was any secrets to be looking for didn't hurt it at all!

+1, would give +10 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A type of clever I didn't expect to see ever be attached to Pattern Screamers. Innovative and executed well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Highly enjoyed the litigious Pattern Screamer in draft form, still enjoy it now. An excellent first article. +1

item.title: Pattern Screamer, esq.
item.content:encoded:
It's a pattern screamer what sues people. I could go on about why I wrote this, but I won't unless someone asks. I wrote it over the course of a feverish night, edited it over a couple days, and then left it sitting until I remembered that it still existed.

In the pattern screamer SCP tradition, there's not-so-subtle backstory hidden in the formatting.

I might expand on the proceedings with a Tale later on, depending on interest (both yours and mine). Stay tuned!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I was worried about that. I will probably work on cutting the practicing lawyer part down a little, since it's really not essential to the ultimate point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is strangely compelling despite the relatively dry writing style. I feel like it could be trimmed a lot in the middle to make the story come more to the forefront; but even in it's current state, I ended up enjoying it quite a lot. In particular, the ending is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The page was incomplete due to some ListPages issues. It's done now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept of presenting an SCP through the guise of a legal document, this could be a really good format screw - however, there doesn't seem to really be anything here about the anomaly itself or why the US government wanted its documentation gone. Novoting for now.

Edit: Following the addition of the actual meat of the article, +1
